sentences.text: Mm-hmm.
I met her on the boat.
Because she was-- she volunteered to take care of the children, to take them to Sweden.
She was a-- at that time, a teacher, later on-- she taught science in a gymnasium.
And she became the principle of that gymnasium later on.
She never married.
She came from a very wealthy family.
She had adopted a young girl from Finland, whose parents had been killed.
Not Jewish, no.
And, in addition to that, she also had a young man, who-- an orphan, a Swedish orphan, that the family had adopted.
So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she also told me that if Hitler had invaded Sweden, her family was on the hit list.
They would've been the first ones to go.
They were act-- because they were very active in the resistance movement that whole time.
Very anti-Nazi, very pro-Jewish.
Sweden was great.
There was no prejudice at all.
Sweden-- Swedish people have a reputation of being aloof, like, or cold.
And I didn't find that so at all.
I was welcome with open arms by everyone I came in contact with.
Yeah.
I was quarantined in Holsbybrunn for about six weeks.
And then this Mrs Dandenell, Frau Dandenell wrote a letter to the authority at Holsbybrunn.
She didn't know my name, but she described me and knew that I--where I was from in Germany, that she wanted-- if I wanted to, she would like me to come and live with her in Stockholm.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Which I did.
But I asked her once, why did she choose me out of these 400 kids?
And she said she liked my smile.
So that was the only reason.
[LAUGHS] And once I was able to leave Holsbybrunn, I took the train to Stockholm.
How were you feeling at that point?
I was physically well.
I was still trying to find my parents.
I had gone to the Red Cross and made inquiries.
They set up these services in Holsbybrunn, because all of us were in the same boat.
You know, we all had lost everybody at home.
We didn't know where they were.
And I also tried to find Dita again, through this Red Cross.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I hadn't found anyone when I went to Stockholm.
So I come to-- came to Stockholm.
The woman picked me up at the railroad station.
And she--I went to her apartment.
And I had a room of my own for the first time in, I don't know, five years?
And a bed with white sheets and as much to eat as I wanted to eat.
What was it like?
Oh, it was just incredible.
I mean, I can still recall the feeling now.
I mean, I really did appreciate it.
And I never went to bed, not once, without stretching out and feeling the luxury of lying in a bed, between two clean sheets, for a long, long time.
I just- It was just great.
And then I had this tremendous desire to learn.
And I don't-- I still don't know why.
It was just like an obsession with me.
So since I had-- my disability was that I didn't speak Swedish.
So she first got a tutor, so that I started learning Swedish.
And they decided they're not going to speak German at all.
So I had to Swedish.
There was no-- Did she speak German?
Yes.
But we only spoke Swedish from that time on.
And I got a-- was- I had a tutor.
Then she had a tutor for me in most subjects, because, I mean, I lost a lot of school in between.
And then she sent me to summer school for a whole summer, which meant that I was only one grade behind by that point.
Yes, she paid for everything.
And the summer school was on an island in the North Sea.
Excuse me.
We were only-- it was all women.
We had one male teacher.
But we all-- we could swim in the nude, because this one male teacher was not allowed at the dock when we went swimming.
And it was just heaven.
It was just beautiful.
And we had classes six hours a day.
And the rest of the time, we swam, we went boating, we went riding.
It was just great, really, that somehow I recovered, just being there.
I gained weight back.
I felt healthy, I felt well.
I was able to do things.
I-- a great appreciation for life, for everything, for beauty.
And to this day, I remember going to this summer camp, the end of May, early June--
This was in '45-- no, '46, sorry.
When the spring is just beginning in Sweden, because spring is late, and I had this feeling of everything awakening.
And you know, when I returned in September, everything was-- fall was already there.
And everything was-- and at-- and to this day, I can remember this feeling of wonder, great appreciation of being able to do this.
And this woman never, ever, in all the years that I lived with her, asked anything of me.
She let me be me, and she let me be free.
She never told me, you have to do this, you have to do that.
She might suggest you should take-- do this or that.
But she never said go to church with us, celebrate Christmas with us, ever, at any time.
Later on, in Stockholm, she made it her business to find a Jewish organization for me, yes.
No.
I had only Swedish friends.
Oh, my friend Hilda, from Theresienstadt, was in Stockholm for a while.
Through this Jewish organization I went to.
And we did spend some time together.
Well, it was great to find her, great that she was alive.
But you're such different people, even now, I mean-- we have-- we have some of our past in common, but that's about it.
You know, we are completely different people.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] And she is the typical kind of woman who would be very happy to settle down, get married and have children.
And that would be her life, which is not enough for me.
So it was just-- I didn't see that much of her.
Basically, my contact was with Swedish people.
And my friends were Swedes.
How did you find Dita?
Red Cross finally came across, and Dita was able to contact me in Stockholm.
And she was living in London at the time, going to nursing school, where she became a nurse.
No, she never did.
Later on, she did come to Canada, but that's later.
And I went to school in Sweden, and I loved every-- every moment I spent in Sweden.
And I realize what this woman has done for me.
And I don't think there are very many people on this earth that will do what she did, without-- no personal gain whatsoever, just because she wanted to be a kind human being.
So she put you through the university?
She put me through college and university there, through gymnasium, through private schools, through summer schools, through everything.
What did you study at the university?
Well, I majored in chemistry.
But I never actually did anything with it.
Well, then, this is it.
My relatives decided the only survivor-- they finally found me.
Mm-hmm.
Also through the Red Cross.
And this is the only survivor on both sides of the family.
I can't stay in Sweden.
I have to come to the United States.
Yeah.
So I decided, well, I'll come to the United States and see what it's like.
And if I don't like it, I'll go back to Sweden.
So I came in December of '49.
By boat, a boat called Drottningholm.
And I was on the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it was in the winter, terrible, terrible storm.
And I was the only one who wasn't seasick.
I was the only one left in the dining room.
I remember that.
[LAUGHS] And I came with my skis.
And here were my relatives, and here they see me.
And I never forget the expression.
Like, they were talking German.
They said, she's crazy.
What does she think she's going to do here with skis?
I mean, we have to work here.
They were poor at this time.
I mean, we can't ski here.
So anyhow, I went home with-- they decided I should live with my one uncle for a while.
Where does he live?
In Washington Heights.
And I stayed for one month.
Because all of a sudden, they decided I had to be home at a certain time-- I could do this, I couldn't do this-- when I had been on my own, all these years.
So I said, no way.
So I got a furnished room, and I moved out.
And then I decided I'd go to City College at night.
Were you working when you were first there?
I was working, yeah.
I got a job at a button factory because I was trying to learn English.
But it turned out, really all I could have learned was Spanish because I was the only non Puerto Rican person there.
And- But I did stay for about three months or so, to make the money.
And I babysat at night, and I went to school in the afternoon.
I worked like part-time, half in the morning, school afternoon, babysat at night, sometimes school at night.
They were not really my family.
I had nothing in common with them.
[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] There's-- they were-- you see, they felt, because I was the only survivor, and because my mother was their sister or my father was their brother, but there was no real love.
I didn't know them.
They didn't know me.
It's-- I'm fond of my aunt now.
But that came because I mean, I've learned to know her again.
I only have one aunt left that's alive.
But-- and I'm fond of one or two cousins.
But basically, they're not-- they really were not my family.
I lived by myself.
Mm hm.
And then I decided to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] oh, then I went-- by that time, then it was winter again, and I wanted to ski.
So I couldn't afford to ski.
So somebody said, why don't you come and work in Lake Placid for a winter.
So I worked at the Hotel Belmont, which is no longer in existence, as a waitress.
And I had to serve breakfast and dinner, and I had the whole day to ski.
And I babysat at night.
And at this time, I came back to New York with over $1,200.
And that was a lot of money then.
What did you do when you came back?
I went back to school.
And then I was contacted by this family in New Rochelle.
The mother needed some-- and I don't even recall what the operation was-- but she needed some sort of operation.
So she asked me, would I stay and take care of the house and her two children when she was gone?
Mm hm.
They had known someone in my family.
That's how I got to them.
So they were paying me a very good salary.
And besides that, and I had-- they hired someone to live in the attic, so I could go to school at night.
So I could-- I didn't have to be there at night.
So I did that.
And I was there for three months.
And then the young woman who lived up on the top floor was the secretary of Maxwell Dane, who became my boss for my first job, for Doyle Dane Bernbach.
That was an advertising agency.
Right.
So this is how it all--
Well, then when we-- when after the three months were up, I had to look for a place to live.
And so this woman, Nell, said, well why don't we share an apartment?
So we rented a two-bedroom apartment in New Rochelle and commuted to New York.
And I started working at Doyle Dane Bernbach.
And when we were 14-- 16 people, I believe, including myself.
And I was like a-- I did everything.
And it was like that became my family.
That became my second family, more than anything else.
And at that point, I wrote to Sweden, and told them that I would stay here.
Oh, I was in contact until this woman died a year ago.
I was in contact with them, yeah.
I have-- I visited her two or three times.
And she has been here to visit me.
Mm hm.
Well, she shaped my life.
She did more for me than anyone has ever done and didn't ask for anything in return.
Now how often does that happen?
At Doyle Dane Bernbach, I met my husband.
He was the auditor for, for a-- of an outside firm.
And I was doing, at that time, I was doing cost control.
And I had to work with him.
And we were married within three months.
Charles Mendelson.
That was in '52.
Yeah, I knew him three months, and I married him.
Mm hm.
And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I became pregnant immediately, almost immediately.
But I really wanted children very, very badly.
Because by that time, I knew that my family had-- that no one survived.
And that I was really the only and I, I just thought that-- the only thing I really, it's up to me now, to make sure that the family doesn't just disappear.
So I, I had-- my daughter was born in April of '54.
Jill.
And I stopped working for six years, because my son was born in April '57.
And then when Dan was three years old and was able to go to nursery school, I went back to work part-time, back to Doyle Dane Bernbach.
By that time, the firm had about 100 some odd employees.
And I worked part time, until, I think, for three years.
And I had a woman at home taking care of the kids, which meant that I really didn't earn any money.
The money that I made went to pay for child care and things like that.
I did.
I loved it.
I loved every moment of my job-- of the work with Doyle Dane Bernbach, while it was still the firm that it was.
And most of-- we were friends.
We did things outside the office.
And it was just like an extended family.
It was good.
And I was-- then after I started working full time, I became more and more involved in my job.
My children always came first.
I mean, I don't want to say that I didn't do that.
But I did become more involved in my job.
My marriage turned bad.
Things began to happen.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think I put more emphasis on work than on anything else.
Now the happiest day in my whole life that I ever had was the day my daughter was born.
And this is an experience I don't think you can-- I mean, I think probably most mothers had the same experience.
But it's really, it's something horrendous, to have this baby that part's of you.
And I tried to teach my children [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] above all, to be true to themselves, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and not to become part of a pack, to be individuals.
I gave them the love for the out of doors, because that was very important to me.
Yes.
And they do that.
And they now go camping with their children.
So somehow it's carried on, from one generation to the other.
You still ski?
I ski, I skate, I swim.
I swim three times a week.
I'm the treasurer for the Adirondack Mountain Club, for the New York chapter.
I'm very active in the Appalachian Mountain Club.
And I'm very active in the [?
Miramar ?] ski club.
I went on a hiking trip to Nepal.
I spent 23 days camping in the mountains and hiking.
That was about four years ago.
And I also worked in Michigan for my firm.
I had to set up the office there in Troy, Michigan.
The, the scars from the concentration camp is I had terrible, terrible depressions, to the point where I tried to commit suicide once.
I've been on antidepressants for years.
And it was a reoccurring depression every year and started with-- when my kids graduated college.
Actually, Jill got married, Dan graduated college.
And that's when it's--the whole thing began.
And I never faced the-- I never faced the Holocaust.
It was something that happened, but I was not involved.
I looked at it as an outsider.
And it's only recently that I really-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I, I hate the people who did this.
And not just the Germans.
I hate the Pope for not speaking up.
I hate Roosevelt for having a quota.
I hate the German Jews, my relatives, for not doing enough, and the American Jews.
More should have been done.
So you can't just blame one people.
There are some-- people started, but how about the rest of the world?
And this is why I feel very strongly about what's happening now.
I think we are responsible for our fellow human beings.
And it is our responsibility, as human beings, to help other human beings.
And it doesn't matter whether they are black or white or Jews or Christian or Hindus or Muslims, or whatever they are.
It's just one fellow human being to another.
And the Holocaust would not have happened, if all the people that so righteously now point their fingers had done something.
And that's not just the Germans.
So- And all this came just recently.
And it's the last two and a half years that I have not been depressed.
Well, going to psychiatrist helped to some extent.
I think it's just facing it myself.
The Holocaust itself?
I had a very-- I never talked to them about it, because I was not part of it.
I was an outsider.
I never spoke to them about it.
Yeah, when they were little, I told them it was my telephone number because otherwise I couldn't remember it.
But now my, my son wants me to take my granddaughter to the museum in Washington.
And-- so it's, it's a different--
Yeah, I was there for the dedication because I was part of it.
I gave testimony there.
Yeah.
It means that all I can do at this point-- you know, it's- it's done with.
I can't change anything.
I can't-- dead people can't be brought back.
And just don't let it happen again.
And that means in any sort or any form.
I mean, I think we have to learn to live peacefully with one another [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] let hate and all that kind of stuff.
Just live as, live as human beings.
Because being a human being means that you should treat your fellow human being as you would like to be treated yourself.
And that, that to me, is the most important thing.
I'm not religious at all.
I am very consciously Jewish.
I'm very proud of my heritage.
I don't-- I believe in a supreme being, but I don't believe in organized religion at all.
Well, I think it's man made.
I mean, you're asking my opinion.
I think all religions are man made.
But I think there is some supreme being.
I mean, I'm not an atheist.
But this supreme being is so horrendous that my little mind could not even under-- begin to understand it.
So I just feel that it's my-- I have to be responsible to myself and to my conscience.
And it's my ethics, more than anything else, then that keep me or make me the person I am.
No one knew, basically.
When I first came here, I have never seen a black person in my life.
And I had always had this idea of this great, free land, with liberty and justice for all.
And I soon found out differently, boy.
There was not the same liberty and justice for all.
And there never has been.
What happened in the South.
And I came in the McCarthy, McCarthy era, and all this was going on.
Then I couldn't vote yet.
But Adlai-- Adlai Stevenson was running for president, he was this great guy.
Yeah, I did, as soon as I could.
Well, I was stateless.
You know, the Germans took away my citizenship after the war.
Well so I decided this was going to be my country, I'm going to live here.
But I don't think there's a country in the world worth dying for, I can tell you that.
Not one.
So I just feel that, if you want to fight, then put all the leaders that want to fight in a circle and let them fight.
I just don't believe in war.
Or any of this, or hate, or prejudice.
No, no.
I was born in Germany, but I'm not a German.
And when I go back to Germany now-- because I have cousins there, from this aunt who was married to a Catholic.
She-- her- her- my uncle died.
She still lives there.
She had five children.
The five children are all Catholics, because they had to be baptized and convert when Hitler came, which didn't help them anyhow.
But my cousin's daughter is leaning towards Judaism now.
And I'm very close to this cousin.
There were first four brothers and one daughter.
And I'm very close to Liesl.
As a matter of fact, she's coming to the United States and going cross country with me next summer.
And I was with her just recently.
And we went hiking.
But when I'm in Germany, I'm not no longer a European.
I'm an American.
We think differently, we are different, believe me.
And I'm not a German or a European.
Well, I'm talking about values now.
My-- I have different values.
I look at things differently.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I look at people-- now people my age or younger had-- I can't hate them, I can't-- I can't blame them.
Some of them, they either weren't born or they were small children.
But I look at some of the older people and I say, gee, I wonder what you were doing.
All I can tell you, no one will ever put me anywhere where I don't want to be.
I mean, that I can tell you.
I mean, I would fight and die before that would happen again.
Nobody knew anything.
I remembered the good days.
Because--
My early childhood.
Like I went to Hellenthal, my grandfather's house is still there.
The house that I was born is still there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went to the cemetery.
There's a war memorial, where my two uncles who died fighting for Germany in World War I, they put their names.
They had taken the names off, they put the names back.
My nurse just died recently.
The woman who took care of me when I was a baby, she lived there.
And I went to visit her.
Yeah.
And so she, well she was-- she had-- she did what she had to do.
She was never-- I mean, she stayed with us as long as she could.
But then it was just-- but she was never a Nazi or anything like that.
And I went back to Hochneukirch.
And the house that my-- that I spent many summers with my grandparents with.
The house was given back to us, by the German-- after, after the war.
And we-- we gave the house to my aunt.
Because she was-- they were very, poor.
She had the five kids.
And I stayed with her in that house, in the garden.
And the garden is still there.
And everything is still there.
Yes.
Well, these really were good-- those were my good memories.
They were, they were not bad.
Bielefeld or Lippstadt, I don't know anymore.
I mean, it changed so, it became so big, that I don't even know where-- I don't, don't know my way.
Stockholm has changed too, completely different.
The first time I went been back, and I saw this room that in my mind was this castle, it's like a little, little tiny room, with a bed and a bookcase and a desk.
And to me, that room was heaven.
It's just what you remember.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But the worst thing that happened-- and I'm bringing this up because it really is-- I was in a ski accident, in an automobile accident.
We came back from skiing, this friend of mine and I.
This was well, we skied in Vermont.
And we went to Massachusetts, because I have a house in the Berkshires.
It's in Lanesborough, it's near Tanglewood, in the Berkshires.
So we decided to stay at my house overnight, before we came back to New York because-- to shorten the drive.
And we left that morning.
That was on January 29th.
What year?
Four years ago.
It will be five years this January.
And it was snowing.
And there was ice on the road.
And I was driving on route 22.
And I started skidding.
And this truck skid, and the truck hit me.
The car turned around completely.
Then he hit again, the passenger's side, full force, and Erica was killed instantly.
And this is my very, very best-- the best friend I ever had.
Not Dita, Dita is my-- was like a sister, more.
And a rip punctured her heart, and she was killed instantly.
And I was in the hospital for a long time.
I was also completely messed up.
But having to live with that, I think, is the-- is the worst thing that ever happened to me.
Worse than anything.
Because I was an adult.
It was different.
Also, it was-- it happened while I was there.
My parents and all this, that was gradual.
This was bang.
It just happened.
This was a woman who had everything to live for.
She was a great skier, a great hiker, a good human being.
We had plan-- we had plans to go skiing in Finland a week after this happened.
And it just-- and I had to go back to a psychiatrist.
And then my depression started all over again.
So that's when I-- that's why I actually went to Nepal.
Because I felt that this is something she would have liked to have done.
And I was going to do it for both of us.
